collodion:craftinginfo-collodion-text: Collodion Photography This guide walks you through the steps of taking your first wet plate photograph. 1) Tools & Chemicals Before you can expose any photographs, you'll need some equipment: • Field Camera • Development Tray • Rough Glass Plates • Collodion • A piece of cloth • Silvering Solution • Plate Box • Developer • Fixer 2) Prep Work Your plates need to be prepared in order to become sensitive to light: • Start by using the cloth to polish your plate into a clean glass plate. • Using a bucket, pour the syrupy collodion binder onto the cleaned plate to make it a collodion-coated plate. • Pour silvering solution over the coated plate to receive a sensitized plate that's ready for exposure. *Note: After sensitizing, the plate will begin to dry up. A dried plate can't be exposed or developed; store your sensitized plates in a Plate Box to preserve them until the moment you're ready to expose them.* The Plate Box will also help you keep your inventory light when out taking pictures. 3) Load & Expose • With a sensitized plate in your offhand, hold the camera and use shift + rightmousebutton to load the plate into it. • Once you're ready to take a photograph, look through the viewfinder rightmousebutton and click leftmousebutton to begin exposing the plate to light. You can control how much light hits the plate by exposing for shorter or longer durations. The optimal exposure time for a collodion plate is about 8-10 seconds. Make sure to keep the camera still until the exposure is complete or the photo may turn out blurry! Click leftmousebutton again to stop the exposure. • Unload the camera the same way to get your newexposed plate. There's still work to do before you can see how it came out. 4) Develop & Fix • Place the exposed plate in your development tray. • As you repeatedly pour developer onto the plate, you should begin to see a negative image form on the surface. • Finalize the process by pouring on fixer to stabilize the plate as a finished photo plate. If your plates ever dry out, they can be recycled and prepped again. Place a dried plate in the developer tray and pour water over it to wash most of the dried chemicals off of the surface. The plate will still be covered in residue and will need to be repolished before it can be recoated and sensitized again. 5) Display • To properly view the photo, a plate needs to be placed in front of a dark background. Place down a hanging or standing frame and mount your plate inside to admire your work! 6) Tripods • Tripods can be attached to a field camera similarly to loading a sensitized plate. This allows you to line up your shot in the viewfinder and set the camera down instead of starting the exposure with the camera in your hands. • You can use a camera mounted on a tripod for steadier long exposures, multiple exposures of the same shot, or even self portraits!
